From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

3-race horse honor in various countries

For the American Triple Crown, see [Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing (United States)](/wiki/Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing_(United_States) "Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing (United States)").

Horse race

Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [Ormonde](/wiki/Ormonde_(horse) "Ormonde (horse)"), an undefeated English Triple Crown winner | |
| Inaugurated | 1853 (173 years ago) (1853) |

The **Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing**, often shortened to **Triple Crown**, is a series of [horse races](/wiki/Horse_racing "Horse racing") for [Thoroughbreds](/wiki/Thoroughbred "Thoroughbred"), often restricted to three-year-olds. Winning all three of these [Thoroughbred horse races](/wiki/Thoroughbred_racing "Thoroughbred racing") is considered the greatest accomplishment in Thoroughbred racing. The term originated in mid-19th-century England and nations where Thoroughbred racing is popular, each having their own Triple Crown series.

## England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: England")]

In [England](/wiki/Horse_racing_in_Great_Britain "Horse racing in Great Britain"), where the term Triple Crown originated with [West Australian](/wiki/West_Australian_(horse) "West Australian (horse)")'s three wins in 1853, it is made up of:

1. The [2,000 Guineas Stakes](/wiki/2000_Guineas_Stakes "2000 Guineas Stakes"), run over 1 mile (1,609 metres) at [Newmarket Racecourse](/wiki/Newmarket_Racecourse "Newmarket Racecourse") in [Newmarket](/wiki/Newmarket,_England "Newmarket, England"), [Suffolk](/wiki/Suffolk "Suffolk")
2. [The Derby](/wiki/Epsom_Derby "Epsom Derby"), run over 1 mile 4 furlongs and 10 yards (2,423 metres) at [Epsom Downs Racecourse](/wiki/Epsom_Downs_Racecourse "Epsom Downs Racecourse") in [Epsom](/wiki/Epsom "Epsom"), [Surrey](/wiki/Surrey "Surrey")
3. The [St Leger Stakes](/wiki/St_Leger_Stakes "St Leger Stakes"), run over 1 mile 6 furlongs and 132 yards (2,937 metres) at [Town Moor](/wiki/Doncaster_Racecourse "Doncaster Racecourse") in [Doncaster](/wiki/Doncaster "Doncaster"), [Yorkshire](/wiki/Yorkshire "Yorkshire")

Since the 2,000 Guineas was first run in 1809, fifteen horses (including three winners of substitute races at Newmarket during the First World War) have won the English Triple Crown. The most recent – and only winner since World War II – was [Nijinsky](/wiki/Nijinsky_II "Nijinsky II"), in 1970. For many years, it was considered unlikely that any horse would ever win the English Triple Crown again. In the winter of 2006/2007, however, trainer [Jim Bolger](/wiki/Jim_Bolger_(racehorse_trainer) "Jim Bolger (racehorse trainer)") was training his unbeaten colt [Teofilo](/wiki/Teofilo_(horse) "Teofilo (horse)") for the Triple Crown[[1]](#cite_note-1) and bookmaker [William Hill plc](/wiki/William_Hill_plc "William Hill plc") was offering odds of only 12/1 on Teofilo winning the 2007 Triple Crown. The horse was withdrawn from the 2000 Guineas two days before the race after suffering a setback and never raced again.

Since Nijinsky, only [Nashwan](/wiki/Nashwan "Nashwan") (1989), [Sea the Stars](/wiki/Sea_the_Stars "Sea the Stars") (2009) and [Camelot](/wiki/Camelot_(horse) "Camelot (horse)") (2012) have won both the Guineas and the Derby. Between [Reference Point](/wiki/Reference_Point_(horse) "Reference Point (horse)") in 1987 and [Camelot](/wiki/Camelot_(horse) "Camelot (horse)") in 2012, no Derby winner (not even the potential Triple Crown winners Nashwan and Sea the Stars) even entered the St. Leger. This reluctance to compete in the St. Leger is said to be because of the impact it would have on a horse's [stud](/wiki/Stud_(animal) "Stud (animal)") value in a market where speed is preferred to stamina.

### Triple Tiara

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Triple Tiara")]

Main article: [American Triple Tiara of Thoroughbred Racing](/wiki/American_Triple_Tiara_of_Thoroughbred_Racing "American Triple Tiara of Thoroughbred Racing")

There have been several different versions of the *Triple Tiara* (sometimes known as the Filly Triple Crown) in the United States. One of them was a national version that consisted of undercard events on the same weekends as the associated Triple Crown races:

1. [Kentucky Oaks](/wiki/Kentucky_Oaks "Kentucky Oaks"), run over 1+1⁄8 miles (1811 m) on a dirt track, at Churchill Downs;
2. [Black-Eyed Susan Stakes](/wiki/Black-Eyed_Susan_Stakes "Black-Eyed Susan Stakes"), run over 1+1⁄8 miles (1811 m) (previously 1+1⁄16 mi or 1.7 km) on a dirt track, at Pimlico Race Course;
3. [Acorn Stakes](/wiki/Acorn_Stakes "Acorn Stakes"), run over 1 mile (1609 m) (previously 1+1⁄2 mi or 2.4 km) on a dirt track, at Belmont Park;

Only one filly won this version of the Triple Tiara, [Davona Dale](/wiki/Davona_Dale "Davona Dale") in 1979. Few have even tried as the short time between the Kentucky Oaks and Black-Eyed Susan is generally considered too short for fillies.

The most commonly accepted version of the Triple Tiara is the [American Triple Tiara of Thoroughbred Racing](/wiki/American_Triple_Tiara_of_Thoroughbred_Racing "American Triple Tiara of Thoroughbred Racing") which uses three races from New York. From 1957 to 2002, and 2007 to 2009, these three races were the [Acorn Stakes](/wiki/Acorn_Stakes "Acorn Stakes"), the [Mother Goose Stakes](/wiki/Mother_Goose_Stakes "Mother Goose Stakes"), and the [Coaching Club American Oaks](/wiki/Coaching_Club_American_Oaks "Coaching Club American Oaks"). Eight fillies won this version of the New York Triple Tiara:

* [Dark Mirage](/wiki/Dark_Mirage "Dark Mirage") – 1968
* [Shuvee](/wiki/Shuvee "Shuvee") – 1969
* [Chris Evert](/wiki/Chris_Evert_(horse) "Chris Evert (horse)") – 1974
* [Ruffian](/wiki/Ruffian_(horse) "Ruffian (horse)") – 1975
* [Davona Dale](/wiki/Davona_Dale "Davona Dale") – 1979
* [Mom's Command](/wiki/Mom%27s_Command "Mom's Command") – 1985
* [Open Mind](/wiki/Open_Mind_(horse) "Open Mind (horse)") – 1989
* [Sky Beauty](/wiki/Sky_Beauty "Sky Beauty") – 1993

In 2010, the NYRA changed the configuration of the Triple Tiara to include the [Alabama Stakes](/wiki/Alabama_Stakes "Alabama Stakes") instead of the Mother Goose. As of 2022, no filly has won the reconfigured Triple Tiara.

### New York Handicap Triple

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: New York Handicap Triple")]

Main article: [New York Handicap Triple](/wiki/New_York_Handicap_Triple "New York Handicap Triple")

The New York Handicap Triple is a series of three handicap races run in New York.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Although historically notable, the series is now essentially defunct, as two of the races are run on the same day, making a sweep impossible. In addition, only the Metropolitan Handicap maintains a top-level designation and continues to be run as a handicap. The series consists of:

1. [Metropolitan Handicap](/wiki/Metropolitan_Handicap "Metropolitan Handicap"), run over 1 mile (1609 m) on a dirt track, at Belmont Park;
2. [Brooklyn Handicap](/wiki/Brooklyn_Handicap "Brooklyn Handicap") (now run as the Brooklyn Invitational Stakes), run over 1+1⁄4 miles (2012 m) (now 1+1⁄2 miles (2414 m)) on a dirt track, at Belmont Park;
3. [Suburban Handicap](/wiki/Suburban_Handicap "Suburban Handicap") (now run as the Suburban Stakes), run over 1+1⁄4 miles (2012 m) on a dirt track, at Belmont Park;

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Tom_Fool_%28USA%29.jpg/250px-Tom_Fool_%28USA%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tom_Fool_(USA).jpg)

Tom Fool, winner of the New York Handicap Triple in 1953

The triple has been won by four horses:

* [Whisk Broom II](/wiki/Whisk_Broom_II "Whisk Broom II") – 1913[[5]](#cite_note-5)
* [Tom Fool](/wiki/Tom_Fool "Tom Fool") – 1953[[6]](#cite_note-6)
* [Kelso](/wiki/Kelso_(horse) "Kelso (horse)") – 1961[[7]](#cite_note-7)
* [Fit to Fight](/wiki/Fit_to_Fight "Fit to Fight") – 1984[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Turf Triple Series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Turf Triple Series")]

In 2019, the [New York Racing Association](/wiki/New_York_Racing_Association "New York Racing Association") established two series of races for three-year-olds on the turf: the Turf Trinity and the Turf Tiara.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) As of 2022, neither has been swept by a singular horse.

The Turf Trinity consists of:

1. [Belmont Derby](/wiki/Belmont_Derby "Belmont Derby"), run over 1+1⁄4 miles (2012 m) on a turf track at Belmont Park
2. [Saratoga Derby Invitational Stakes](/wiki/Saratoga_Derby_Invitational_Stakes "Saratoga Derby Invitational Stakes"), run over 1+3⁄16 miles (1911 m) on a turf track at Saratoga
3. [Jockey Club Derby](/wiki/Jockey_Club_Derby "Jockey Club Derby"), run over 1+1⁄2 miles (2414 m) on a turf track at Belmont Park

The Turf Tiara consists of:

1. [Belmont Oaks](/wiki/Belmont_Oaks "Belmont Oaks"), run over 1+1⁄4 miles (2012 m) on a turf track at Belmont Park
2. [Saratoga Oaks Invitational Stakes](/wiki/Saratoga_Oaks_Invitational_Stakes "Saratoga Oaks Invitational Stakes"), run over 1+3⁄16 miles (1911 m) on a turf track at Saratoga
3. [Jockey Club Oaks](/wiki/Jockey_Club_Oaks "Jockey Club Oaks"), run over 1+3⁄8 miles (2213 m) on a turf track at Belmont Park

## Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Ireland")]

The Irish Triple Crown, modelled on the English equivalent,[[11]](#cite_note-11) consists of:

1. [Irish 2,000 Guineas](/wiki/Irish_2,000_Guineas "Irish 2,000 Guineas"), run over 1 mile (1609 m) on a turf track at the [Curragh](/wiki/Curragh_Racecourse "Curragh Racecourse")
2. [Irish Derby](/wiki/Irish_Derby "Irish Derby"), run over 1+1⁄2 miles (2414 m) on a turf track at the Curragh
3. [Irish St. Leger](/wiki/Irish_St._Leger "Irish St. Leger"), run over 1+3⁄4 miles (2816 m) on a turf track at the Curragh

For a list of the annual individual race winners, see [Irish Triple Crown race winners](/wiki/Irish_Triple_Crown_race_winners "Irish Triple Crown race winners").

Only two horses have won all three races since the Irish Two Thousand Guineas was first run in 1921:

* Museum – 1935
* Windsor Slipper – 1942

## Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Australia")]

Main article: [Australian Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing](/wiki/Australian_Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing "Australian Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing")

The Australian Triple Crown comprises the following races:[[15]](#cite_note-15)

1. [Randwick Guineas](/wiki/Randwick_Guineas "Randwick Guineas"), run over 1600 metres (0.99 mi) on a turf track at [Randwick Racecourse](/wiki/Randwick_Racecourse "Randwick Racecourse")
2. [Rosehill Guineas](/wiki/Rosehill_Guineas "Rosehill Guineas"), run over 2000 metres (1.2 mi) on a turf track at [Rosehill Gardens Racecourse](/wiki/Rosehill_Gardens_Racecourse "Rosehill Gardens Racecourse")
3. AJC [Australian Derby](/wiki/Australian_Derby "Australian Derby"), run over 2400 metres (1.5 mi) on a turf track at Randwick Racecourse

The Australian Triple Crown initially included the [Canterbury Guineas](/wiki/Canterbury_Guineas "Canterbury Guineas"), which was replaced with the Randwick Guineas.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### The Spring Grand Slam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: The Spring Grand Slam")]

Main article: [Spring Grand Slam](/wiki/Spring_Grand_Slam "Spring Grand Slam")

The Spring Grand Slam for older horses consists of:

1. [Caulfield Cup](/wiki/Caulfield_Cup "Caulfield Cup"), run over 2400 metres (1.5 mi) on a turf track at [Caulfield Racecourse](/wiki/Caulfield_Racecourse "Caulfield Racecourse")
2. [Cox Plate](/wiki/Cox_Plate "Cox Plate"), run over 2040 metres (1.27 mi) on a turf track at [Moonee Valley Racecourse](/wiki/Moonee_Valley_Racecourse "Moonee Valley Racecourse")

## New Zealand

## France

The French Triple Crown consists of:

1. [Poule d'Essai des Poulains](/wiki/Poule_d%27Essai_des_Poulains "Poule d'Essai des Poulains") (French 2000 Guineas), run over 1600 metres (0.99 mi) on a turf track at [Longchamp Racecourse](/wiki/Longchamp_Racecourse "Longchamp Racecourse")

### Japanese Dirt Triple Crown

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Japanese Dirt Triple Crown")]

The Japanese Dirt Triple Crown is run by the [NAR](/wiki/National_Association_of_Racing "National Association of Racing") instead of the [JRA](/wiki/Japan_Racing_Association "Japan Racing Association"). Most dirt racing in Japan is run under the NAR. In 2022 the NAR announced an official Dirt Triple Crown that includes 3 pre-existing domestic Grade 1 races, all three of which are run on dirt tracks.[[22]](#cite_note-:26-22) These are:

* the [Haneda Hai](/wiki/Haneda_Hai "Haneda Hai"), run over 1800 metres (1.1 mi)
* the [Tokyo Derby](/wiki/Tokyo_Derby_(horse_race) "Tokyo Derby (horse race)"), run over 2000 metres (1.2 mi)
* the [Japan Dirt Classic](/wiki/Japan_Dirt_Classic "Japan Dirt Classic"), run over 2000 metres (1.2 mi)

All three races are held at [Oi Racecourse](/wiki/Oi_Racecourse "Oi Racecourse") in [Shinagawa](/wiki/Shinagawa "Shinagawa"), [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), Japan. Although the Triple Crown did not debut until 2024, the three races comprising it have all been run since 1999. 3 horses have thus retroactively won a Dirt Triple Crown:

| Year | Winner | Jockey | Trainer | Owner | Breeder |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1999 | Orion The Thanks | Hideharu Hayata | Kiyomatsu Akama | Keiko Hiura | Takanori Morinaga |
| 2001 | Toshin Blizzard | Takayuki Ishizaki | Kenji Sato | Eiichi Izumi | Kazue Muranaka |
| 2023 | Mick Fire | Norifumi Mikamoto | Kazuo Watanabe | Koichi Hoshika | Takahashi Farm |

## Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Brazil")]

Brazil has triple crowns run at multiple tracks, including at [Hipódromo da Gávea](/wiki/Hip%C3%B3dromo_da_G%C3%A1vea "Hipódromo da Gávea") (Rio de Janeiro) and at Hipódromo de Cidade Jardim (São Paulo).[[45]](#cite_note-:9-45)

The Rio de Janeiro Triple Crown consists of:[[45]](#cite_note-:9-45)

1. Grande Prêmio Estado do Rio de Janeiro, run over 1600 metres (0.99 mi) on a turf track
2. Grande Prêmio Francisco Eduardo de Paula Machado, run over 2000 metres (1.2 mi) on a turf track
3. [Grande Prêmio Cruzeiro do Sul](/wiki/Grande_Pr%C3%AAmio_Cruzeiro_do_Sul "Grande Prêmio Cruzeiro do Sul") (Brazilian Derby), run over 2400 metres (1.5 mi) on a turf track

Winners are:

* Talvez! – 1941[[46]](#cite_note-:20-46)
* Criolan – 1942[[46]](#cite_note-:20-46)
* Quiproquó – 1953[[47]](#cite_note-:16-47)
* Timão – 1956[[47]](#cite_note-:16-47)
* Escorial – 1959[[48]](#cite_note-48)
* African Boy – 1979[[49]](#cite_note-49)
* Old Master – 1984[[50]](#cite_note-50)
* [Itajara](/wiki/Itajara_(horse) "Itajara (horse)") – 1987[[51]](#cite_note-51)
* Groove – 1996[[52]](#cite_note-52)
* Super Power – 2000[[53]](#cite_note-53)
* Plenty of Kicks – 2012[[54]](#cite_note-54)
* [Bal a Bali](/wiki/Bal_a_Bali "Bal a Bali") – 2014[[55]](#cite_note-55)

Prior to 1963,[[56]](#cite_note-:21-56) the Rio de Janeiro Triple Crown consisted of the Grande Prêmio Outono[[57]](#cite_note-57) (1600 metres (0.99 mi) on turf[[46]](#cite_note-:20-46)), Grande Prêmio Cruzeiro do Sul, and Grande Prêmio Distrito Federal (3000 metres (1.9 mi) on turf).[[46]](#cite_note-:20-46) From 1963 to 1993, it consisted of the Grande Prêmio Estado do Rio de Janeiro, Grande Prêmio Cruzeiro do Sul, and Grande Prêmio Jockey Club Brasileiro (3200 metres (2.0 mi) on turf).[[56]](#cite_note-:21-56) From 1994 to 2003, the order of the Grande Prêmio Jockey Club Brasileiro and Grande Prêmio Cruzeiro do Sul were reversed in order.[[58]](#cite_note-58) The current configuration started in 2004.

The Rio de Janeiro Filly Triple Crown consists of:[[45]](#cite_note-:9-45)

1. Grande Prêmio Henrique Possolo, run over 1600 metres (0.99 mi) on a turf track
2. Grande Prêmio Diana, run over 2000 metres (1.2 mi) on a turf track
3. Grande Prêmio Zélia Gonzaga Peixoto de Castro, run over 2400 metres (1.5 mi) on a turf track

Winners are:

* Indian Chris – 1991[[59]](#cite_note-59)
* Virginie – 1997[[60]](#cite_note-60)
* Be Fair – 2000[[61]](#cite_note-61)
* Old Tune – 2012[[62]](#cite_note-62)
* No Regrets – 2017[[63]](#cite_note-63)
* Janelle Monae – 2021[[64]](#cite_note-64)

The São Paulo Triple Crown consists of:[[45]](#cite_note-:9-45)

1. Grande Prêmio Ipiranga, run over 1600 metres (0.99 mi) on a turf track
2. Grande Prêmio Jockey Club de São Paulo, run over 2000 metres (1.2 mi) on a turf track
3. [Grande Prêmio Derby Paulista](/wiki/Grande_Pr%C3%AAmio_Derby_Paulista "Grande Prêmio Derby Paulista"), run over 2400 metres (1.5 mi) on a turf track

Winners are:

* Jacutinga – 1933[[65]](#cite_note-:18-65)
* Funny Boy – 1936[[66]](#cite_note-:22-66)
* El Faro – 1943[[66]](#cite_note-:22-66)
* Estouvado – 1944[[66]](#cite_note-:22-66)

The São Paulo Filly Triple Crown consists of:[[45]](#cite_note-:9-45)

## Chile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Chile")]

## Panama

### Crown Races

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Crown Races")]

The races that make up the Triple Crown in Panama are held at the Hipódromo Presidente Remón and are as follows:

1. Primera Gema: **Clásico Arturo, Eric, Max, Eric Arturo, and Eric Antonio Delvalle** (Grade 2) (1800 m)

## Hong Kong

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Hong Kong")]

The Triple Crown series at [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong")'s [Sha Tin Racecourse](/wiki/Sha_Tin_Racecourse "Sha Tin Racecourse") consists of three races at increasingly longer distances. Unlike most other Triple Crown events, these races are not confined to three-year-olds.[[97]](#cite_note-97) They are:

1. [Hong Kong Stewards' Cup](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Stewards%27_Cup "Hong Kong Stewards' Cup"), run over 1600 metres (0.99 mi)
2. [Hong Kong Gold Cup](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Gold_Cup "Hong Kong Gold Cup"), run over 2000 metres (1.2 mi)
3. [Hong Kong Champions & Chater Cup](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Champions_%26_Chater_Cup "Hong Kong Champions & Chater Cup"), run over 2400 metres (1.5 mi)

There have been two horses to win the Hong Kong Triple Crown:

* River Verdon – 1994
* Voyage Bubble – 2025

There are two other Triple Crown series: the Hong Kong Speed Series and the Four-Year-Old Classic Series.

**Hong Kong Speed Series** (International Group 1):

1. [Centenary Sprint Cup](/wiki/Centenary_Sprint_Cup "Centenary Sprint Cup"), run over 1200 metres (0.75 mi)
2. [Queen's Silver Jubilee Cup](/wiki/Queen%27s_Silver_Jubilee_Cup "Queen's Silver Jubilee Cup"), run over 1400 metres (0.87 mi)
3. [Chairman's Sprint Prize](/wiki/Chairman%27s_Sprint_Prize "Chairman's Sprint Prize"), run over 1200 metres (0.75 mi)

Winners of the Hong Kong Speed Series are:

* Mr. Vitality – 1995/96
* Grand Delight – 2002/03
* [Silent Witness](/wiki/Silent_Witness_(horse) "Silent Witness (horse)") – 2003/04, 2004/05
* Lucky Sweynesse – 2022/23
* [Ka Ying Rising](/wiki/Ka_Ying_Rising "Ka Ying Rising") – 2024/2025[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/HKIR_20231210_Golden_Sixty.jpg/250px-HKIR_20231210_Golden_Sixty.jpg)](/wiki/File:HKIR_20231210_Golden_Sixty.jpg)

  Golden Sixty, winner of the Hong Kong Four-Year-Old Classic Series in 2020

**Four-Year-Old Classic Series** (Domestic Group 1):

Restricted to four-year-old horses.

1. [Hong Kong Classic Mile](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Classic_Mile "Hong Kong Classic Mile"), run over 1600 metres (0.99 mi)
2. [Hong Kong Classic Cup](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Classic_Cup "Hong Kong Classic Cup"), run over 1800 metres (1.1 mi)
3. [Hong Kong Derby](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Derby "Hong Kong Derby"), run over 2000 metres (1.2 mi)

Winners of the Four-Year-Old Classic Series are:

* [Rapper Dragon](/wiki/Rapper_Dragon "Rapper Dragon") – 2017
* [Golden Sixty](/wiki/Golden_Sixty "Golden Sixty") – 2020

## South Korea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: South Korea")]

The current Triple Crown series that started in 2008 consists of:

1. Korea Mile Cup run over 1600 meters at Busan-Gyeongnam Racecourse
2. Korean Derby, Run over 1800 meters at LetsRun Park Seoul
3. The Minister of Agriculture Cup over 2000 meters at LetsRun Park Seoul

Currently only one horse has swept this modern version of the Triple Crown

Originally the Triple Crown was started a year prior in 2007 and consisted of:

1. Ttukseom Cup, run over 1400 meters at LetsRun Park Seoul
2. Korean Derby, Run over 1800 meters at LetsRun Park Seoul
3. The Minister of Agriculture Cup over 2000 meters at LetsRun Park Seoul

In the only year it was run it produced a Triple Crown winner

1. J.S Hold[[99]](#cite_note-99)

## Uruguay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Uruguay")]

The three races that compose the Triple Crown in Uruguay are:[[26]](#cite_note-:0-26)

1. [Gran Premio Polla de Potrillos](/wiki/Gran_Premio_Polla_de_Potrillos_(Uruguay) "Gran Premio Polla de Potrillos (Uruguay)"), run over 1600 metres (0.99 mi) on a dirt track
2. [Gran Premio Jockey Club](/wiki/Gran_Premio_Jockey_Club_(Uruguay) "Gran Premio Jockey Club (Uruguay)"), run over 2000 metres (1.2 mi) on a dirt track
3. [Gran Premio Nacional](/wiki/Gran_Premio_Nacional_(Uruguay) "Gran Premio Nacional (Uruguay)"), run over 2500 metres (1.6 mi) on a dirt track

This combination of races received some publicity outside of Uruguay in 2006. The 2005 Triple Crown winner [Invasor](/wiki/Invasor_(horse) "Invasor (horse)"), after being sold to Sheik [Hamdan bin Rashid Al Maktoum](/wiki/Hamdan_bin_Rashid_Al_Maktoum "Hamdan bin Rashid Al Maktoum")'s [Shadwell Racing](/wiki/Shadwell_Racing "Shadwell Racing") and sent to be raced in the United States, went on to win three [Grade I](/wiki/Graded_stakes_race "Graded stakes race") races in 2006 before winning that year's [Breeders' Cup Classic](/wiki/Breeders%27_Cup_Classic "Breeders' Cup Classic"). He finished the year as the top-ranked horse in the 2006 [World Thoroughbred Racehorse Rankings](/wiki/World_Thoroughbred_Racehorse_Rankings "World Thoroughbred Racehorse Rankings"), and won the 2007 [Dubai World Cup](/wiki/Dubai_World_Cup "Dubai World Cup") before being retired to stud following a training injury.

### Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Russia")]

The Russian Triple Crown differs from other Triple Crowns in a major way by instead having its three jewels be spread out over three years. The Triple Crown consists of:[[107]](#cite_note-107)

1. Grand Prize (1600m for 2-year-olds)
2. Grand All Russian Derby (2400m for 3-year-olds)
3. Prize of the Minister of Agriculture (3200m for 4-year-olds)

Currently only three horses have won this version of the Triple Crown

* Budynok – 1930
* Grog II – 1950
* Anilin – 1965

There is a more traditional 3-year-old triple crown in Russia modeled after the English Triple Crown but no horse has ever won all three races. But if they did they would have to win;

1. Grand Summer Prize (1600m Russian 2,000 Guineas)
2. Grand All Russian Derby (2400m)
3. S.M. Budyonny Prize (2800m Russian St.Leger)

3. [Indonesia Derby](/wiki/Indonesia_Derby "Indonesia Derby"), Dirt 2000m

Indonesian Triple Crown winners are:

* Mystere (colt) – 1978[[122]](#cite_note-kuda-122)[[a]](#cite_note-123)
* Manik Trisula (filly) – 2002
* [Djohar Manik](/wiki/Djohar_Manik "Djohar Manik") (filly) – 2014
* [King Argentin](/wiki/King_Argentin "King Argentin") (colt) – 2025

Note that thoroughbred horses are not eligible to run at the Indonesian Triple Crown, since the event is limited to only local breeds like [Sandalwood Pony](/wiki/Sandalwood_Pony "Sandalwood Pony"), crossbreeds (known locally as *G Horses*), or *Kuda Pacu Indonesia*/KPI (crosses between crossbreeds).[[123]](#cite_note-124) No Triple Crown for thoroughbred racing is organized by [PORDASI](/wiki/PORDASI "PORDASI"), as races exclusively for thoroughbreds are only held in two non-derby classes.

### Philippines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing&action=edit&section=69 "Edit section: Philippines")]

Main article: [Philracom Triple Crown](/wiki/Philracom_Triple_Crown "Philracom Triple Crown")

The Triple Crown Stakes backed by the [Philippine Racing Commission](/wiki/Philippine_Racing_Commission "Philippine Racing Commission") (Philracom).[[124]](#cite_note-125) It was first organized in 1978, but a horse did not sweep all three races until 1981, when [Fair and Square](/wiki/Fair_and_Square_(horse) "Fair and Square (horse)") achieved that feat, becoming the series' first Triple Crown champion.[[125]](#cite_note-kickedoff-126)

The races are held at various venues throughout its history. The first two legs of the series have traditionally been referred to as the Cojuangco Cup and the J.V. Ongpin Cup, respectively, while the final leg has been known as the Horseman's Cup.[[126]](#cite_note-127)[[127]](#cite_note-128) However, recent editions commonly refer to the races simply by their order as legs of the Triple Crown series.

Its winners include:[[125]](#cite_note-kickedoff-126)[[128]](#cite_note-two-down-bashierro-129)

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| * Fair and Square – 1981 * Skywalker – 1983 * Time Master – 1987 * Magic Showtime – 1988 * Sun Dancer – 1989 * Strong Material – 1996 | * Real Top – 1998 * Silver Story – 2001 * [Hagdang Bato](/wiki/Hagdang_Bato "Hagdang Bato") – 2012 * Kid Molave – 2014 * [Sepfourteen](/wiki/Sepfourteen "Sepfourteen") – 2017 * Heneral Kalentong – 2020 |

In 2025, the [Metro Manila Turf Club](/wiki/Metro_Manila_Turf_Club "Metro Manila Turf Club") introduced two new Triple Crown‑style stakes races: the Prince Cup and the King's Gold Cup. Each tournament consists of three legs and is restricted to horses foaled outside the Philippines.[[129]](#cite_note-130) The first editions are still ongoing and hence no horse has made a sweep in either tournaments.[[130]](#cite_note-131)

### Trinidad and Tobago

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing&action=edit&section=70 "Edit section: Trinidad and Tobago")]

The three races that compose the triple crown of Trinidad and Tobago were established in 1983 and they are:[[26]](#cite_note-:0-26)

1. Easter Guineas
2. Midsummer Classic Stakes
3. Trinidad Derby[[131]](#cite_note-132)

The Trinidad and Tobago triple crown winners are[[132]](#cite_note-133)

Pre 1983:

* Bright Light – 1952
* Darjeeling – 1954
* Shalimar – 1958
* Happy Landing – 1960
* Aurelian – 1963
* Chip Chip – 1967
* [Royal Colours](/wiki/Royal_Colours "Royal Colours") – 1982

After 1983:

* Sky Rocket – 1986
* Carnival Messiah – 2001
* Momentum – 2014
* Wise Guy – 2020
* In The Headlines – 2023
* [Headliner](/wiki/Headliner_(horse) "Headliner (horse)") – 2025

## Individual Triple Crown winners

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing&action=edit&section=72 "Edit section: Individual Triple Crown winners")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Eddie_Arcaro.jpg/120px-Eddie_Arcaro.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eddie_Arcaro.jpg)

Eddie Arcaro, winner of the American Triple Crown twice

Only three jockeys have won the Triple Crown with different horses (i.e., rode horses to Triple Crowns in different years):

* [Steve Donoghue](/wiki/Steve_Donoghue "Steve Donoghue") (United Kingdom : 1915, Pommem; 1917 Gay Crusader)
* [Eddie Arcaro](/wiki/Eddie_Arcaro "Eddie Arcaro") (United States: 1941, Whirlaway; 1948, Citation)
* Emisael Jaramillo (Venezuela: 2005, Polo Grounds; 2007, Taconeo; 2010, Water Jet)

At least two jockeys is known to have won all three of a country's Triple Crown races in the same year on different horses:

* [Luis Contreras](/wiki/Luis_Contreras_(jockey) "Luis Contreras (jockey)") (Canada, 2011: Queen's Plate, Inglorious; Prince of Wales Stakes and Breeders' Stakes, Pender Harbour)
* Alfredo García Paduani (Venezuela, 2016: Clásico José Antonio Páez and Clásico Cría Nacional, Ocean Bay; Clásico República Bolivariana de Venezuela, Gran Will)

One trainer is known to have accomplished the same feat as Contreras and García Paduani:

* [D. Wayne Lukas](/wiki/D._Wayne_Lukas "D. Wayne Lukas") (USA, 1995: Kentucky Derby, Thunder Gulch; Preakness Stakes, Timber Country; Belmont Stakes, Thunder Gulch)
